Name: mutual_information_based_compression_framework
Title: A Mutual Information-Based Framework for Model Compression and Generalization
Short Hypothesis: We hypothesize that a framework integrating mutual information metrics with rate-distortion theory can provide tighter bounds on model compression efficiency while preserving generalization capabilities across various tasks.
Related Work: Previous works, such as those by Sefidgaran et al. and Isik et al., have explored the relationships between generalization, compression, and mutual information. However, they often treat these domains separately or only in specific contexts. Our proposal distinguishes itself by providing a unified framework that explicitly connects these concepts through a common mathematical lens, enhancing the interpretability and applicability of derived bounds.
Abstract: In this proposal, we aim to develop a novel framework that integrates mutual information metrics with rate-distortion theory to address model compression in machine learning. Our hypothesis is that understanding the interplay between model complexity, generalization error, and task-specific performance can lead to tighter bounds on compression efficiency. By framing model compression as a rate-distortion problem, we will derive achievable rates and distortion-rate curves that account for the mutual information between input data and model predictions. We will also explore how bits allocation based on task-relevant information can optimize compression while maintaining predictive sufficiency. Our approach will leverage empirical evaluations to validate theoretical predictions, ultimately contributing to a deeper understanding of how to balance model size and performance across different applications.
Experiments: 1. Conduct a series of experiments on benchmark datasets (e.g., MNIST, CIFAR-10) to evaluate the proposed framework against traditional compression methods.
2. Measure the mutual information between model parameters and predictive outcomes to derive the optimal bits allocation for compression.
3. Compare generalization errors across varying levels of model compression using established metrics (e.g., accuracy, F1 score) to validate theoretical predictions.
4. Analyze the distortion-rate curves obtained from the experiments to evaluate the efficiency of the proposed method relative to existing techniques.
Risk Factors and Limitations: Potential risks include the complexity of deriving empirical measures of mutual information in high-dimensional spaces and the challenge of ensuring that the framework remains computationally feasible. Additionally, while the theoretical predictions are grounded in established principles, empirical validation may show unforeseen discrepancies in specific contexts.
